title: FE Mechanical
subtitle: Fundamentals of Engineering - Computer-Based Test
tagline: Everything you need to pass the FE Mechanical exam: structured lessons, worked practice, quizzes, and full practice tests across all 14 knowledge areas.

examFacts.passNote: No fixed passing percentage - NCEES uses a criterion-referenced cut score (community estimates around 50-60% correct). No penalty for wrong answers, so never leave a blank.

guide.overview: Mathematics is the toolkit every other section borrows from: analytic geometry, vectors, calculus (derivatives, integrals, basic differential equations), and linear algebra. On the FE you rarely derive anything — you recognize the form and execute fast.
objectives: Work fluently with vectors: components, dot and cross products, magnitudes and angles.
Differentiate and integrate standard functions; apply the chain, product, and quotient rules.
Recognize and solve first-order and simple second-order differential equations.
Solve small linear systems using substitution, Cramer's rule, or matrices.
guide.prereqs: Algebra, trigonometry, and the standard calculus sequence.
watchOuts: Radians vs. degrees — set your calculator correctly before trig.
Dropping the constant of integration when an initial condition is given.
Cross product gives a vector (with direction); dot product gives a scalar.
Sign errors when resolving vectors into components.

keyEquations.name: Integration by parts
keyEquations.formula: INT u dv = uv - INT v du

lesson.heading: Vectors are your workhorse
lesson.body: A vector has magnitude and direction. Resolve it into components with Ax = A cos(t) and Ay = A sin(t), then recombine with |A| = sqrt(Ax^2+Ay^2) and t = atan2(Ay, Ax). The dot product A.B = |A||B|cos(t) tells you how aligned two vectors are (zero means perpendicular). The cross product AxB is perpendicular to both, with magnitude |A||B|sin(t) - used constantly for moments (M = r x F) and areas.

lesson.heading: Calculus you actually need
lesson.body: Memorize the derivative and integral of polynomials, exponentials, logs, and the six trig functions. The chain rule d/dx f(g(x)) = f'(g)*g'(x) shows up everywhere. For integrals, recognize the form first: power rule, u-substitution, or integration by parts. Definite integrals give area, accumulated change, average value, and centroids.

lesson.heading: Differential equations on the FE
lesson.body: Most FE ODEs are first-order linear (dy/dx + Py = Q) or separable. For y' = ky the solution is y = y0*e^(kt) - growth/decay. Simple second-order constant-coefficient equations (ay'' + by' + cy = 0) are solved with the characteristic equation a*r^2+b*r+c = 0; the roots tell you whether the response is overdamped, critically damped, or oscillatory.

lesson.heading: Linear algebra, fast
lesson.body: For 2x2 and 3x3 systems, Cramer's rule is quick: x_i = det(A_i)/det(A). The determinant of a 2x2 [[a,b],[c,d]] is ad - bc. A zero determinant means no unique solution. Matrix multiplication is row-times-column; it is not commutative.

practiceProblems.prompt: Given A = 3i + 4j and B = 1i + 2j, find A.B (the dot product).
practiceProblems.explanation: Dot product = AxBx + AyBy = (3)(1) + (4)(2) = 3 + 8 = 11. Remember the dot product returns a scalar.

practiceProblems.prompt: What is the derivative of f(x) = x^2 * sin(x)?
practiceProblems.explanation: Use the product rule (uv)' = u'v + uv'. With u = x^2 (u' = 2x) and v = sin(x) (v' = cos x): f' = 2x*sin(x) + x^2*cos(x).

practiceProblems.prompt: Evaluate the definite integral of 3x^2 from 0 to 2.
practiceProblems.explanation: The antiderivative of 3x^2 is x^3. Evaluate from 0 to 2: (2)^3 - (0)^3 = 8.

practiceProblems.prompt: The general solution of dy/dt = 0.5y is:
practiceProblems.explanation: This is exponential growth: dy/dt = ky gives y = y0*e^(kt). Here k = 0.5, so y = y0*e^(0.5t).

quiz.prompt: |AxB| for |A|=5, |B|=2, angle 30 deg between them:
The determinant of [[2,1],[4,3]] is:
d/dx [ln(3x)] =
quiz.explanation: d/dx ln(3x) = (1/3x)*3 = 1/x by the chain rule.

quiz.prompt: The integral of cos(x) dx =
quiz.explanation: The antiderivative of cos(x) is sin(x) + C.

guide.overview: A compact, high-yield section: descriptive statistics (mean, median, standard deviation), basic probability rules, and the normal distribution. Most problems are plug-and-chug if you know which formula to grab.
objectives: Compute mean, median, mode, variance, and standard deviation.
Apply addition and multiplication rules; handle independent and mutually exclusive events.
Use the normal distribution and z-scores.
Recognize permutations vs. combinations.
watchOuts: Population vs. sample standard deviation (dividing by N vs. N-1).
Confusing 'and' (multiply) with 'or' (add) in probability.
Permutations count order; combinations do not.

keyEquations.name: Addition rule
keyEquations.formula: P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A and B)

lesson.heading: Describing data
lesson.body: The mean is the average; the median is the middle value (robust to outliers); the mode is most frequent. Standard deviation measures spread. Use n-1 in the denominator for a sample, n for a full population - the FE usually specifies. Variance is the square of standard deviation.

lesson.heading: Probability rules
lesson.body: For 'or' events use the addition rule P(A or B) = P(A)+P(B)-P(A and B); if mutually exclusive the overlap is zero. For 'and' with independent events, multiply: P(A and B) = P(A)P(B). Conditional probability is P(A|B) = P(A and B)/P(B).

lesson.heading: Counting
lesson.body: Permutations P(n,r) = n!/(n-r)! count ordered arrangements. Combinations C(n,r) = n!/[r!(n-r)!] count selections where order does not matter. Ask yourself: does swapping two items create a new outcome? If yes, permutation.

lesson.heading: The normal distribution
lesson.body: Convert any value to a z-score z = (x-mu)/sigma, then read the standard-normal table (provided in the handbook). Remember the 68-95-99.7 rule: about 68% of data lies within +/-1 sigma, 95% within +/-2 sigma, 99.7% within +/-3 sigma.

practiceProblems.prompt: Find the mean of 4, 8, 10, 14.
A fair die is rolled. P(rolling a 2 OR a 5)?
practiceProblems.explanation: Mutually exclusive events: P = 1/6 + 1/6 = 2/6 = 1/3.

practiceProblems.prompt: How many ways to choose 2 people from 5 (order doesn't matter)?
For mu=50, sigma=5, what is the z-score of x=60?
practiceProblems.explanation: z = (x-mu)/sigma = (60-50)/5 = 2. The value is two standard deviations above the mean.

quiz.prompt: Two independent events have P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.5. P(A and B)?
quiz.explanation: Independent 'and' means multiply: 0.4 * 0.5 = 0.2.

quiz.prompt: The median of 3, 7, 9, 12, 100 is:
quiz.explanation: Median is the middle of the sorted list: 9. It is unaffected by the outlier 100.

quiz.prompt: Variance is:
choices: the square root of std dev
quiz.explanation: Variance = (standard deviation)^2.

quiz.prompt: Permutations differ from combinations because they:
quiz.explanation: Permutations count ordered arrangements; combinations ignore order.

guide.overview: Pure points if you internalize one principle: the engineer's paramount duty is to protect public health, safety, and welfare. Questions test the NCEES/NSPE code, professional obligations, conflicts of interest, and basic agency ideas.
objectives: State the engineer's paramount obligation (public safety).
Identify conflicts of interest and how to handle them.
Apply rules on competence, confidentiality, and honest representation.
Recognize when to act as a faithful agent or trustee.
guide.prereqs: None - read the NSPE Code of Ethics once.
watchOuts: When in doubt, the answer almost always protects the public.
Engineers must only work in their area of competence.
Disclose conflicts of interest; never accept gifts that compromise judgment.

keyEquations.name: Paramount duty
keyEquations.formula: Public health, safety & welfare > client/employer interest

keyEquations.name: Competence
keyEquations.formula: Practice only in areas of qualification

lesson.heading: The hierarchy of obligations
lesson.body: Engineers hold paramount the safety, health, and welfare of the public. This outranks duty to client, employer, and self. If an exam answer pits 'finish on schedule' or 'save the client money' against 'public safety,' choose public safety every time.

lesson.heading: Competence and honesty
lesson.body: Perform services only in your areas of competence. Issue public statements only in an objective and truthful manner. Avoid deceptive acts. Sign and seal only work you have prepared or directly supervised.

lesson.heading: Conflicts of interest
lesson.body: Disclose all known or potential conflicts to clients or employers. Do not accept compensation from more than one party for the same project unless all parties consent. Do not accept gifts or favors that could influence professional judgment.

lesson.heading: Faithful agent
lesson.body: Act for each employer or client as a faithful agent or trustee. Keep client information confidential. Do not solicit or accept work on a contingent basis that would compromise judgment.

practiceProblems.prompt: An engineer discovers a design flaw that could endanger the public, but fixing it will delay the project. The engineer should:
choices: Stay silent to meet the deadline
Report the flaw and ensure it is addressed
practiceProblems.explanation: Public safety is paramount and outranks schedule. The engineer must report and ensure the flaw is corrected.

practiceProblems.prompt: An engineer is asked to stamp drawings prepared by someone they did not supervise. They should:
choices: Refuse unless they reviewed/supervised the work
practiceProblems.explanation: Engineers may seal only work they prepared or directly supervised. Stamping unreviewed work is unethical.

practiceProblems.prompt: Accepting a valuable gift from a vendor bidding on your project is:
choices: A conflict of interest to avoid or disclose
Acceptable if the bid is lowest
practiceProblems.explanation: Gifts that could influence judgment create a conflict of interest and must be avoided or disclosed.

practiceProblems.prompt: An engineer is offered a project outside their expertise. The ethical action is:
choices: Take it and learn as you go
Decline or associate with a qualified expert
practiceProblems.explanation: Engineers must work only within their competence - decline or bring in qualified expertise.

quiz.prompt: The engineer's paramount duty is to:
quiz.explanation: Protecting public health, safety, and welfare is the paramount obligation.

quiz.prompt: Confidential client information should be:
quiz.explanation: Engineers act as faithful agents and keep client information confidential.

quiz.prompt: Public statements by engineers must be:
quiz.explanation: Engineers must be objective and truthful in public statements.

quiz.prompt: Working outside your area of competence is:
quiz.explanation: Practicing outside your competence violates the code unless you associate with qualified experts.

guide.overview: Time value of money. Master the factor notation in the handbook and these become near-automatic: present worth, future worth, annuities, and simple comparisons. High reward for low effort.
objectives: Move money across time with P, F, and A factors.
Read and apply the (F/P, i, n) style factor tables.
Compare alternatives using present worth and annual cost.
Handle simple vs. compound interest and effective rates.
watchOuts: Match the interest rate period to the number of periods (monthly i with monthly n).
Effective annual rate is not the nominal rate when compounding more than once a year.
Read whether a value is given as present (now) or future (later).

lesson.heading: The core idea: money has a time value
lesson.body: A dollar today is worth more than a dollar later because it can earn interest. Every problem is about shifting cash flows to a common point in time - usually 'now' (present worth P) or 'the end' (future worth F) - using the interest rate i and number of periods n.

lesson.heading: Single payments
lesson.body: To push a present amount forward: F = P(1+i)^n. To pull a future amount back: P = F(1+i)^-n. The FE handbook lists these as factors written (F/P, i%, n) and (P/F, i%, n) - you just multiply the known amount by the tabulated factor.

lesson.heading: Uniform series (annuities)
lesson.body: When equal payments A occur each period, use the capital-recovery factor to convert a present amount into payments (A/P), or the sinking-fund factor to build a future amount (A/F). Loan payments, depreciation, and recurring costs all use these.

lesson.heading: Comparing alternatives
lesson.body: Bring every option's cash flows to the same basis - present worth or equivalent annual cost - then pick the better number (lowest cost or highest worth). Make sure compounding periods match the payment periods before you compute.

practiceProblems.prompt: You invest $1,000 at 6% annual compound interest for 5 years. Future value?
What is the present worth of $5,000 received 4 years from now at 8%?
A nominal 12% rate compounded monthly has an effective annual rate of about:
Simple interest on $2,000 at 5% for 3 years equals:
practiceProblems.explanation: Simple interest I = P*i*n = 2000(0.05)(3) = $300 (no compounding).

quiz.prompt: F = P(1+i)^n converts:
quiz.explanation: It pushes a present amount P forward to a future amount F.

quiz.prompt: If compounding is monthly, n for 3 years is:
quiz.explanation: Monthly periods over 3 years: 3 * 12 = 36.

quiz.prompt: To compare two projects fairly you should:
choices: bring cash flows to a common time basis
quiz.explanation: Convert all cash flows to a common basis (present worth or annual cost) before comparing.

quiz.prompt: Effective annual rate exceeds nominal rate when:
choices: compounding is more than once per year
quiz.explanation: More frequent compounding raises the effective rate above the nominal rate.

guide.overview: DC and basic AC circuits: Ohm's law, series/parallel resistance, Kirchhoff's laws, power, capacitors and inductors, and simple AC impedance. Mechanical candidates often under-study this - don't.
objectives: Apply Ohm's law and the power relations.
Reduce series and parallel resistor networks.
Use Kirchhoff's voltage and current laws.
Handle capacitor/inductor energy and basic AC impedance.
guide.prereqs: Algebra and complex numbers for AC.
watchOuts: Resistors in parallel: the equivalent is smaller than the smallest resistor.
Watch units: mA, kOhm, microF.
In AC, use RMS values for power unless told otherwise.

lesson.heading: Ohm's law and power
lesson.body: The foundation: V = IR. Power dissipated by a resistor is P = VI, and by substitution P = I^2 R = V^2/R. Pick the form that uses the quantities you already know.

lesson.heading: Combining resistors
lesson.body: In series, resistances add: Req = R1 + R2 + ... and the same current flows through each. In parallel, 1/Req = sum(1/Ri) and the same voltage appears across each; the equivalent is always less than the smallest branch. For two parallel resistors, Req = R1*R2/(R1+R2).

lesson.heading: Kirchhoff's laws
lesson.body: KCL: the sum of currents into a node equals the sum out (charge is conserved). KVL: around any closed loop the voltage rises equal the drops (energy is conserved). These let you solve any network too complex for simple series/parallel reduction.

lesson.heading: Energy storage and AC
lesson.body: A capacitor stores E = 0.5 C V^2; an inductor stores E = 0.5 L I^2. In AC circuits, capacitors and inductors present frequency-dependent reactance: Xc = 1/(2 pi f C) and Xl = 2 pi f L. Impedance Z combines resistance and reactance as a complex quantity, and power calculations use RMS values.

practiceProblems.prompt: A 12 V source drives a 4 Ohm resistor. The current is:
practiceProblems.explanation: Ohm's law: I = V/R = 12/4 = 3 A.

practiceProblems.prompt: Two resistors, 6 Ohm and 3 Ohm, in parallel give:
practiceProblems.explanation: Req = R1*R2/(R1+R2) = (6*3)/(6+3) = 18/9 = 2 Ohm. Always less than the smaller resistor.

practiceProblems.prompt: Power dissipated by a 10 Ohm resistor carrying 2 A:
Energy stored in a 100 microF capacitor charged to 50 V:

quiz.prompt: Resistors in series:
quiz.explanation: Series resistances add directly: Req = R1 + R2 + ...

quiz.prompt: KCL is a statement of conservation of:
quiz.explanation: Kirchhoff's current law conserves charge at a node.

quiz.prompt: Capacitive reactance Xc as frequency increases:
quiz.explanation: Xc = 1/(2 pi f C) - it decreases as frequency rises.

quiz.prompt: For a 9 V battery and 3 Ohm resistor, power delivered is:

guide.overview: Bodies in equilibrium: force resultants, free-body diagrams, equilibrium of rigid bodies, trusses and frames, centroids and moments of inertia, and friction. One of the largest buckets and the foundation of the solid-mechanics chapters.
objectives: Resolve forces into components; find resultants and moments.
Draw correct free-body diagrams and write equilibrium equations.
Solve trusses by joints and sections; analyze frames and machines.
Locate centroids and compute moments of inertia (including parallel-axis).
Apply static friction (impending motion, F = mu*N).
guide.prereqs: Vectors and trigonometry. Precise free-body diagrams are the whole game.
watchOuts: Incomplete or wrong free-body diagrams (missing a reaction or force).
Moment sign/direction errors; wrong moment arm (perpendicular distance).
Assuming friction is at maximum when motion isn't actually impending.
Forgetting the parallel-axis term when shifting moment of inertia.

keyEquations.name: Equilibrium (2D)
keyEquations.formula: sum Fx = 0, sum Fy = 0, sum M = 0

keyEquations.name: Static friction
keyEquations.formula: F <= mu_s * N (max at impending motion)

lesson.heading: Everything starts with a free-body diagram
lesson.body: Isolate the body, draw every external force and reaction acting on it, and label known dimensions. Most statics errors are FBD errors - a missing reaction, a force in the wrong direction, or a mislabeled angle. Get the FBD right and the three equilibrium equations (sum Fx = 0, sum Fy = 0, sum M = 0) usually finish the problem.

lesson.heading: Moments and the smart pivot
lesson.body: A moment is force times perpendicular distance: M = F*d. Choose your moment center wisely - summing moments about a point where an unknown acts eliminates that unknown from the equation, often solving for a reaction in one step.

lesson.heading: Trusses and frames
lesson.body: For trusses, use the method of joints when a joint has 2 or fewer unknown member forces, or the method of sections (cut through 3 or fewer members) when you want a specific member directly. Two-force members carry force only along their length. Frames and machines have multi-force members; analyze each member with its own FBD.

lesson.heading: Centroids, inertia, and friction
lesson.body: For composite areas, xbar = sum(xi*Ai)/sum(Ai). To find moment of inertia about a non-centroidal axis, add the transfer term: I = Ic + A*d^2. For friction, the resisting force can be anything up to F_max = mu_s*N - it only equals mu_s*N at impending motion, so don't assume maximum friction unless the problem says sliding is about to start.

practiceProblems.prompt: A 10 m simply-supported beam carries a 200 N load 3 m from the left support A. Find R_A.
practiceProblems.explanation: Sum moments about B: R_A(10) - 200(10-3) = 0, so R_A(10) = 1400 and R_A = 140 N. (R_B = 60 N by sum Fy.)

practiceProblems.prompt: A 100 N block sits on a surface with mu_s = 0.3. Horizontal force to initiate motion?
practiceProblems.explanation: Normal force N = 100 N. F_max = mu_s*N = 0.3 * 100 = 30 N.

practiceProblems.prompt: A force of 50 N acts 0.4 m from a pivot, perpendicular. The moment is:
Moment of inertia of an area is shifted a distance d from the centroid. You must add:
practiceProblems.explanation: Parallel-axis theorem: I = Ic + A*d^2. The transfer term is the area times distance squared.

quiz.prompt: For 2D equilibrium, how many independent equations are available?
quiz.explanation: Three: sum Fx = 0, sum Fy = 0, and sum M = 0.

quiz.prompt: A two-force member carries force:
quiz.explanation: Two-force members carry force only along the line connecting the two pins.

quiz.prompt: Method of sections works best by cutting through at most:
quiz.explanation: Cut through 3 or fewer members so the three equilibrium equations can solve them.

quiz.prompt: Static friction force before impending motion is:
choices: less than or equal to mu*N
quiz.explanation: Friction is <= mu_s*N and only reaches mu_s*N at impending motion.

guide.overview: Motion and its causes: kinematics (position, velocity, acceleration), Newton's second law, work-energy and impulse-momentum methods, and simple harmonic vibration. Decide early whether energy or momentum is the faster route.
objectives: Relate position, velocity, and acceleration (constant and variable a).
Apply sum F = ma and the work-energy theorem.
Use impulse-momentum and conservation of momentum.
Analyze simple harmonic motion and natural frequency.
guide.prereqs: Statics (free-body diagrams), calculus (v = dx/dt), and energy concepts.
watchOuts: Mixing up average and instantaneous quantities.
Forgetting that work-energy is a scalar method (no directions to track).
Sign conventions in momentum (velocity direction matters).

lesson.heading: Kinematics first
lesson.body: If acceleration is constant, use v = v0 + at, dx = v0*t + 0.5*a*t^2, and v^2 = v0^2 + 2a*dx. If acceleration varies, fall back to calculus: v = dx/dt, a = dv/dt, and integrate. Projectile motion is just two independent constant-acceleration problems (horizontal a=0, vertical a=-g).

lesson.heading: Newton's second law
lesson.body: Draw the FBD, then apply sum F = ma along each axis. The acceleration points in the direction of the net force. For systems of connected bodies, write sum F = ma for each mass and link them through the shared acceleration or tension.

lesson.heading: Energy vs. momentum - pick the shortcut
lesson.body: Work-energy (W = dKE) is best when you know forces and distances and want a speed; it's scalar, so no angles to chase. Impulse-momentum (F*dt = m*dv) and conservation of momentum shine for collisions and impacts where time or interacting masses matter. Recognizing which to use saves the most time.

lesson.heading: Vibrations
lesson.body: A spring-mass system oscillates at natural frequency wn = sqrt(k/m) rad/s, with f = wn/(2 pi). Adding damping reduces amplitude over time. The FE keeps this basic: find wn, period T = 2 pi/wn, or the frequency.

practiceProblems.prompt: A car accelerates from rest at 3 m/s^2 for 5 s. Final speed?
A 2 kg mass has a net force of 10 N. Its acceleration is:
A 1,000 kg car moving at 20 m/s has kinetic energy:
A spring-mass system with k = 200 N/m and m = 2 kg has natural frequency wn of:
practiceProblems.explanation: wn = sqrt(k/m) = sqrt(200/2) = sqrt(100) = 10 rad/s.

quiz.prompt: Work-energy methods are best when you want:
choices: a speed from forces and distance
quiz.explanation: Work-energy directly relates force, distance, and speed change.

quiz.prompt: In a perfectly inelastic collision, what is conserved?
quiz.explanation: Momentum is always conserved; kinetic energy is not in inelastic collisions.

quiz.prompt: Period of a spring-mass system is:
quiz.explanation: T = 2 pi/wn = 2 pi sqrt(m/k).

quiz.prompt: Projectile horizontal acceleration (no drag) is:
quiz.explanation: Horizontal acceleration is zero; only gravity acts vertically.

guide.overview: How solids respond to load: stress and strain, axial/torsion/bending, the stress-strain curve, beam shear and moment, and combined loading. Builds directly on Statics.
objectives: Compute axial, shear, bearing, and thermal stress/strain.
Apply Hooke's law and use E, G, and Poisson's ratio.
Find bending stress (sigma = Mc/I) and torsional shear (tau = Tr/J).
Sketch shear and moment diagrams.
guide.prereqs: Statics (internal reactions, centroids, I). Know section properties.
watchOuts: Confusing area moment of inertia I with polar moment J.
Using diameter where radius is needed (c, r).
Forgetting thermal strain delta = alpha*L*dT in constrained members.

lesson.heading: Stress and strain
lesson.body: Normal stress sigma = P/A (force over area). Normal strain epsilon = delta/L (deformation over original length). In the elastic region they're proportional via Hooke's law sigma = E*epsilon, where E is Young's modulus. Shear stress tau = V/A and shear strain relate through the shear modulus G.

lesson.heading: The stress-strain curve
lesson.body: Loading a ductile material: a linear elastic region (slope E), then the yield point, plastic deformation, ultimate strength, and finally fracture. The proportional limit ends the straight line; the yield strength is the design-critical value. Poisson's ratio links lateral to axial strain.

lesson.heading: Bending and torsion
lesson.body: A beam in bending develops sigma = Mc/I, where c is the distance from the neutral axis to the outer fiber and I is the area moment of inertia. A shaft in torsion develops tau = Tr/J, where J is the polar moment of inertia. Don't mix up I (bending) and J (torsion), and watch radius vs. diameter.

lesson.heading: Shear and moment diagrams
lesson.body: Start from the support reactions (a statics problem), then track shear V along the beam: point loads cause jumps, distributed loads cause slopes. The moment diagram is the integral of shear - maximum moment occurs where shear crosses zero, and that's usually where bending stress is critical.

practiceProblems.prompt: A 5,000 N axial load acts on a 10 mm^2 cross-section. The stress is:
practiceProblems.explanation: sigma = P/A = 5000 N / (10e-6 m^2) = 5e8 Pa = 500 MPa.

practiceProblems.prompt: A steel rod (E = 200 GPa) has strain 0.001. The stress is:
practiceProblems.explanation: sigma = E*epsilon = (200e9)(0.001) = 2e8 Pa = 200 MPa.

practiceProblems.prompt: In sigma = Mc/I, the term c represents:
practiceProblems.explanation: c is the distance from the neutral axis to the extreme (outer) fiber, where bending stress is largest.

practiceProblems.prompt: A bar of length 2 m, alpha = 12e-6 per C, heated 50 C, free to expand. Elongation?

quiz.prompt: Young's modulus E is the slope of:
quiz.explanation: E is the slope of the linear elastic portion of the stress-strain curve.

quiz.prompt: Torsional shear stress uses which property?
quiz.explanation: tau = Tr/J uses the polar moment of inertia J.

quiz.prompt: Maximum bending moment along a beam typically occurs where:
quiz.explanation: The moment is the integral of shear, so it peaks where shear = 0.

quiz.prompt: Poisson's ratio relates:
quiz.explanation: Poisson's ratio = -(lateral strain)/(axial strain).

guide.overview: Properties (mechanical, thermal, electrical), the structure-property link, phase diagrams and heat treatment, and failure modes like fatigue and creep. More conceptual than computational.
objectives: Distinguish strength, stiffness, ductility, hardness, and toughness.
Read a binary phase diagram (lever rule, eutectic).
Explain heat treatment effects (annealing, quenching, tempering).
Recognize fatigue, creep, and brittle vs. ductile failure.
guide.prereqs: Basic chemistry and the stress-strain curve.
watchOuts: Strength (stress to fail) is not stiffness (resistance to deflection, set by E).
Toughness is area under the stress-strain curve, not hardness.
Fatigue failure happens below the static yield stress under cyclic load.

lesson.heading: Properties vocabulary
lesson.body: Strength is the stress a material withstands before yielding or fracturing. Stiffness is resistance to elastic deflection, governed by E (a stiff material isn't necessarily strong). Ductility is how much it deforms before fracture; brittleness is the opposite. Hardness resists indentation; toughness is the energy absorbed before fracture (area under the stress-strain curve).

lesson.heading: Structure determines properties
lesson.body: Crystal structure (BCC, FCC, HCP), grain size, and defects control behavior. Smaller grains generally mean higher strength (Hall-Petch). Alloying and cold work strengthen metals; they also typically reduce ductility.

lesson.heading: Phase diagrams and heat treatment
lesson.body: A binary phase diagram maps phases vs. composition and temperature. The lever rule gives the fraction of each phase in a two-phase region. Heat treatment changes microstructure: annealing softens and relieves stress; quenching (rapid cooling) hardens steel by forming martensite; tempering then restores some toughness.

lesson.heading: Failure modes
lesson.body: Fatigue is failure under repeated cyclic loading, often well below the static strength - the endurance limit matters for steels. Creep is slow deformation under sustained load at high temperature. Ductile failure shows necking and warning; brittle failure is sudden with little deformation.

practiceProblems.prompt: Stiffness of a material is primarily set by its:
practiceProblems.explanation: Stiffness (resistance to elastic deflection) is governed by Young's modulus E.

practiceProblems.prompt: Quenching steel produces a hard phase called:
practiceProblems.explanation: Rapid cooling (quenching) traps carbon, forming hard, brittle martensite.

practiceProblems.prompt: Failure under repeated cyclic loading below the yield stress is:
practiceProblems.explanation: That's fatigue - cracks initiate and grow under cyclic stress, failing below the static strength.

practiceProblems.prompt: Toughness corresponds to:
choices: the slope of the elastic line
practiceProblems.explanation: Toughness is the energy absorbed before fracture - the area under the stress-strain curve.

quiz.prompt: A material that deforms a lot before breaking is:
quiz.explanation: Large deformation before fracture means ductile.

quiz.prompt: Annealing primarily:
quiz.explanation: Annealing softens the metal and relieves internal stresses.

quiz.prompt: Creep is significant at:
quiz.explanation: Creep is time-dependent deformation at elevated temperature under constant load.

quiz.prompt: The lever rule gives:
choices: phase fractions in a two-phase region
quiz.explanation: The lever rule computes the weight fraction of each phase in a two-phase field.

guide.overview: Fluids at rest and in motion: hydrostatic pressure, buoyancy, continuity, Bernoulli's equation, the energy equation with head loss, and the meaning of Reynolds number. A heavy hitter for mechanical candidates.
objectives: Compute hydrostatic pressure and forces on surfaces.
Apply buoyancy (Archimedes).
Use continuity (A1*V1 = A2*V2) and Bernoulli's equation.
Classify flow with Reynolds number and account for head loss.
guide.prereqs: Statics, energy concepts, and unit fluency.
watchOuts: Gauge vs. absolute pressure.
Bernoulli assumes steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline.
Use consistent units; density of water is about 1000 kg/m^3.

lesson.heading: Fluid statics
lesson.body: Pressure increases linearly with depth: P = rho*g*h (gauge). This drives forces on dam walls, tank sides, and submerged gates - the resultant acts at the centroid of the pressure distribution. Manometers use the same relation across fluid columns. Always note whether pressure is gauge or absolute.

lesson.heading: Buoyancy
lesson.body: Archimedes' principle: the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid, Fb = rho_fluid * g * V_displaced. An object floats when its weight equals the buoyant force at partial submersion; it sinks when its density exceeds the fluid's.

lesson.heading: Continuity and Bernoulli
lesson.body: Mass conservation for incompressible flow gives A1*V1 = A2*V2 - narrower pipe, faster flow. Bernoulli's equation, P + 0.5*rho*V^2 + rho*g*z = constant, trades pressure, kinetic, and potential energy along a streamline. It assumes steady, incompressible, frictionless flow - handy for nozzles, Venturi meters, and pitot tubes.

lesson.heading: Real flow: Reynolds and head loss
lesson.body: Reynolds number Re = rho*V*D/mu predicts the regime: laminar below about 2,300, turbulent above about 4,000 (in pipes). Real pipe flow loses energy to friction; the energy equation adds a head-loss term, often from the Darcy-Weisbach equation with a friction factor read off the Moody chart.

practiceProblems.prompt: Gauge pressure at 5 m depth in water (rho=1000, g=9.81):
Water flows in a pipe that narrows from 0.1 m^2 to 0.05 m^2. If V1 = 2 m/s, V2 =
practiceProblems.explanation: Continuity: A1*V1 = A2*V2, so V2 = (0.1*2)/0.05 = 4 m/s.

practiceProblems.prompt: Buoyant force on a 0.02 m^3 object fully submerged in water:
A Reynolds number of 800 in a pipe indicates flow that is:
practiceProblems.explanation: Re below about 2,300 in a pipe is laminar flow.

quiz.prompt: Bernoulli's equation assumes flow is:
quiz.explanation: Bernoulli applies to steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline.

quiz.prompt: If a pipe's area halves, the velocity:
quiz.explanation: Continuity: smaller area means proportionally higher velocity, so it doubles.

quiz.prompt: Hydrostatic pressure depends on:
quiz.explanation: P = rho*g*h - density and depth only (not the shape or area).

quiz.prompt: Reynolds number compares:
quiz.explanation: Re is the ratio of inertial to viscous forces.

guide.overview: Energy and its transformations: properties and states, the first and second laws, ideal gases, cycles (Carnot, Rankine, refrigeration), and basic psychrometrics for HVAC. Often the single largest topic.
objectives: Apply the first law (energy balance) to closed and open systems.
Use ideal gas relations and property tables.
Compute thermal efficiency and COP for cycles.
Read a psychrometric chart for HVAC basics.
guide.prereqs: Algebra, unit conversions, and reading property tables.
watchOuts: Absolute temperature (K or R) in all gas-law and efficiency formulas.
Sign conventions for heat and work.
COP can exceed 1; efficiency cannot.

lesson.heading: States and properties
lesson.body: A pure substance's state is fixed by two independent intensive properties (e.g., P and T, or P and quality x). Use steam/refrigerant tables to look up internal energy u, enthalpy h, and entropy s. In the two-phase region, properties are interpolated by quality: h = hf + x*hfg.

lesson.heading: First law: conserve energy
lesson.body: For a closed system, Q - W = dU. For an open system (control volume) at steady state, energy in equals energy out, including flow work - leading to the enthalpy form for turbines, pumps, nozzles, and heat exchangers. Track sign conventions: heat in positive, work out positive (in the common convention).

lesson.heading: Ideal gases
lesson.body: PV = mRT (or Pv = RT per unit mass), with T in absolute units. For specific processes - isothermal, isobaric, isochoric, adiabatic - particular relations apply, and du = cv*dT, dh = cp*dT for ideal gases.

lesson.heading: Cycles and HVAC
lesson.body: Power cycles convert heat to work: thermal efficiency eta = W_net/Q_in, capped by the Carnot limit eta = 1 - T_cold/T_hot (absolute temps). Refrigeration and heat pumps are rated by COP = useful heat/work in, which can exceed 1. For HVAC, the psychrometric chart relates dry-bulb temperature, humidity ratio, relative humidity, and enthalpy of moist air.

practiceProblems.prompt: A closed system absorbs 500 J of heat and does 200 J of work. dU =
practiceProblems.explanation: First law: dU = Q - W = 500 - 200 = 300 J.

practiceProblems.prompt: A Carnot engine operates between 600 K and 300 K. Max efficiency?
practiceProblems.explanation: eta = 1 - T_cold/T_hot = 1 - 300/600 = 0.5 = 50%. Temperatures must be absolute.

practiceProblems.prompt: A refrigerator removes 2,000 J from the cold space using 500 J of work. COP =
practiceProblems.explanation: COP = Q_cold/W_in = 2000/500 = 4. COP above 1 is normal for refrigeration.

practiceProblems.prompt: For an ideal gas at constant volume, boundary work done is:
practiceProblems.explanation: Boundary work W = integral of P dV; at constant volume dV = 0, so W = 0.

quiz.prompt: Temperatures in efficiency formulas must be in:
quiz.explanation: Carnot and gas-law relations require absolute temperature.

quiz.prompt: Thermal efficiency of a power cycle is:
quiz.explanation: eta = W_net/Q_in.

quiz.prompt: In the two-phase region, quality x represents:
quiz.explanation: Quality is the fraction of the mixture mass that is vapor.

quiz.prompt: A COP greater than 1 means:
choices: more heat moved than work input
a violation of the first law
quiz.explanation: COP > 1 simply means the heat moved exceeds the work input - perfectly allowed.

guide.overview: The three modes - conduction, convection, radiation - plus fins, the thermal-resistance network, and basic heat exchangers. Treat it like a resistance circuit and it gets much easier.
objectives: Apply Fourier's law for conduction.
Use Newton's law of cooling for convection.
Combine resistances in series/parallel (the thermal circuit).
Understand radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann) and basic heat exchangers.
guide.prereqs: Algebra and the resistance-network analogy from circuits.
watchOuts: Conduction resistance is L/(kA); convection is 1/(hA).
Radiation uses absolute temperature to the 4th power.
Keep area and thickness consistent.

lesson.heading: Three modes
lesson.body: Conduction moves heat through solids by molecular interaction: q = k*A*dT/L (Fourier's law). Convection moves heat between a surface and a moving fluid: q = h*A*dT (Newton's law of cooling). Radiation transfers heat as electromagnetic waves, no medium needed: q = e*sigma*A*T^4, with T absolute.

lesson.heading: The thermal-resistance analogy
lesson.body: Heat flow is like current, temperature difference like voltage, and thermal resistance like electrical resistance: q = dT/R_total. Conduction resistance is R = L/(kA); convection resistance is R = 1/(hA). Layers in series add resistances - perfect for composite walls.

lesson.heading: Fins and lumped systems
lesson.body: Fins add surface area to boost convective heat loss. A lumped-capacitance analysis (valid when the Biot number is small) treats an object as a single temperature that decays exponentially toward the surroundings.

lesson.heading: Heat exchangers
lesson.body: Heat exchangers transfer heat between two fluid streams. The rate is q = U*A*dT_lm, where U is the overall heat transfer coefficient and dT_lm is the log-mean temperature difference. Counterflow arrangements are more effective than parallel flow.

practiceProblems.prompt: A wall: k=0.5 W/m.K, A=10 m^2, L=0.2 m, dT=30 C. Heat rate?
Convection: h=25 W/m^2.K, A=2 m^2, dT=40 C. Heat rate?
Thermal resistance of conduction is:
practiceProblems.explanation: Conduction resistance R = L/(kA).

practiceProblems.prompt: Radiation heat transfer depends on temperature to the:
practiceProblems.explanation: Stefan-Boltzmann: q is proportional to T^4 (absolute temperature).

quiz.prompt: Newton's law of cooling describes:
quiz.explanation: q = h*A*dT is convection (Newton's law of cooling).

quiz.prompt: Composite wall layers in series have resistances that:
quiz.explanation: Series thermal resistances add, just like series electrical resistors.

quiz.prompt: Radiation requires:
quiz.explanation: Radiation needs no medium - it transfers through vacuum.

quiz.prompt: Counterflow heat exchangers are generally:
quiz.explanation: Counterflow maintains a more favorable temperature difference and is more effective.

guide.overview: Sensors and measurement basics (error, calibration, signal conditioning) plus introductory control systems: block diagrams, feedback, transfer functions, and stability concepts.
objectives: Distinguish accuracy, precision, resolution, and error types.
Describe common sensors (thermocouples, strain gauges, pressure transducers).
Reduce simple control block diagrams and find transfer functions.
Understand open vs. closed loop and basic stability.
guide.prereqs: Algebra, basic differential equations, and circuits.
watchOuts: Accuracy (closeness to true value) vs. precision (repeatability).
Closed-loop transfer function = G/(1+GH) for negative feedback.
Systematic error shifts the mean; random error spreads it.

lesson.heading: Measurement fundamentals
lesson.body: Accuracy is how close a reading is to the true value; precision is how repeatable readings are. Resolution is the smallest detectable change. Systematic (bias) errors consistently shift results and can be calibrated out; random errors scatter around the mean and are reduced by averaging.

lesson.heading: Common sensors
lesson.body: Thermocouples produce a voltage from a temperature difference (Seebeck effect). Strain gauges change resistance with deformation, usually read by a Wheatstone bridge. Pressure transducers, RTDs, and accelerometers each convert a physical quantity into an electrical signal that is then conditioned and digitized.

lesson.heading: Control block diagrams
lesson.body: Systems are modeled as blocks with transfer functions in the Laplace domain. Series blocks multiply; parallel blocks add. For a negative-feedback loop with forward gain G and feedback H, the closed-loop transfer function is T = G/(1 + GH).

lesson.heading: Open vs. closed loop and stability
lesson.body: Open-loop control acts without measuring the output; closed-loop (feedback) control corrects based on the measured error, improving accuracy and disturbance rejection. A system is stable if its response stays bounded - in terms of poles, all must lie in the left half of the s-plane.

practiceProblems.prompt: A scale reads 10.1 kg for a true 10.0 kg mass, every time. This is:
practiceProblems.explanation: A consistent offset is a systematic/bias error - it can be calibrated out.

practiceProblems.prompt: For G = 10 and H = 1 (negative feedback), the closed-loop gain is:
A thermocouple measures temperature using the:
practiceProblems.explanation: Thermocouples rely on the Seebeck effect - a voltage from a temperature gradient across dissimilar metals.

practiceProblems.prompt: Series-connected blocks in a block diagram have transfer functions that:
practiceProblems.explanation: Cascaded (series) blocks multiply their transfer functions.

quiz.prompt: Precision refers to:
quiz.explanation: Precision is repeatability; accuracy is closeness to the true value.

quiz.prompt: Closed-loop control uses:
quiz.explanation: Closed-loop control feeds the measured output back to correct error.

quiz.prompt: A strain gauge is typically read with a:
quiz.explanation: Small resistance changes are measured with a Wheatstone bridge.

quiz.prompt: Averaging many readings reduces:
quiz.explanation: Averaging reduces random error; it does not remove systematic bias.

guide.overview: Designing components to carry load safely: factor of safety, static and fatigue failure theories, stress concentrations, and machine elements (bolts, welds, springs, gears, bearings, shafts). Synthesizes statics, mechanics of materials, and materials.
objectives: Apply factor of safety to allowable stress.
Use failure theories (max shear / distortion energy) for static loading.
Account for stress concentration and fatigue (endurance limit).
Size basic machine elements (bolts, springs, gears, shafts).
guide.prereqs: Mechanics of Materials and Material Properties.
watchOuts: Apply stress-concentration factors at notches, holes, and fillets.
Fatigue strength is well below static strength under cyclic load.
Factor of safety = strength / actual stress (keep it consistent).

lesson.heading: Factor of safety
lesson.body: Design components so the material strength comfortably exceeds the actual stress: FS = strength/actual stress, or the allowable stress = strength/FS. Choose FS based on uncertainty, consequences of failure, and load type. A higher factor of safety means a more conservative (heavier, costlier) design.

lesson.heading: Static failure theories
lesson.body: For ductile materials, the distortion-energy (von Mises) theory and the maximum-shear-stress theory predict yielding under combined loading. For brittle materials, the maximum-normal-stress theory applies. Compute the relevant equivalent stress and compare it to the yield (or ultimate) strength.

lesson.heading: Stress concentration and fatigue
lesson.body: Geometric discontinuities - holes, fillets, notches, keyways - amplify local stress by a factor Kt: sigma_max = Kt * sigma_nominal. Under cyclic loading this drives fatigue. Steels show an endurance limit (stress below which infinite life is possible); designing below it, with modifying factors, prevents fatigue failure.

lesson.heading: Machine elements
lesson.body: Bolts are sized for tensile and shear stress and preload. Springs follow F = k*x with helical spring rate k = G*d^4/(8*D^3*N). Gears transmit torque through meshing teeth with a velocity ratio set by tooth counts. Shafts carry combined bending and torsion - designed using a combined-stress or fatigue criterion.

practiceProblems.prompt: A part's material yields at 250 MPa and the actual stress is 100 MPa. Factor of safety?
practiceProblems.explanation: FS = strength/actual stress = 250/100 = 2.5.

practiceProblems.prompt: A hole gives Kt = 2.5 in a member with nominal stress 80 MPa. Peak stress?
practiceProblems.explanation: sigma_max = Kt * sigma_nominal = 2.5 * 80 = 200 MPa at the edge of the hole.

practiceProblems.prompt: A spring with k = 500 N/m is compressed 0.04 m. Force?
The endurance limit is relevant to:
practiceProblems.explanation: The endurance limit is the cyclic stress below which steels can have effectively infinite fatigue life.

quiz.prompt: A higher factor of safety generally yields a design that is:
quiz.explanation: Higher FS means more margin - a more conservative (and usually heavier/costlier) design.

quiz.prompt: Von Mises (distortion energy) theory predicts failure of:
quiz.explanation: The distortion-energy theory is used for ductile material yielding.

quiz.prompt: Stress concentration factor Kt is largest at:
quiz.explanation: Geometric discontinuities concentrate stress, raising Kt.

quiz.prompt: Helical spring rate increases most with:
quiz.explanation: k = G*d^4/(8*D^3*N) - the wire diameter d to the 4th power dominates.

questions.prompt: A 8 V source drives a 2 Ohm resistor. Power dissipated?
A simply supported 8 m beam has a 400 N load at midspan. Each reaction?
questions.explanation: By symmetry each support carries half: 200 N.

questions.prompt: sigma = P/A for 6000 N over 3e-4 m^2:
Gauge pressure at 10 m water depth:
Carnot efficiency between 800 K and 400 K:
Derivative of 4x^3:
FS for 300 MPa strength and 120 MPa stress:
Two 4 Ohm resistors in parallel:
F = ma for m = 5 kg, a = 4 m/s^2:
Continuity: pipe area drops from 0.2 to 0.1 m^2, V1 = 3 m/s. V2?
Conduction q for k=2, A=4 m^2, dT=20, L=0.1 m:
KE of 800 kg car at 30 m/s:
Bending stress sigma = Mc/I; doubling c (others equal):
questions.explanation: sigma is proportional to c, so doubling c doubles stress.

questions.prompt: First law: Q=800 J in, W=300 J out. dU?
Re = rho*V*D/mu; Re = 5000 indicates:
questions.explanation: Re above about 4000 in pipes is turbulent.

questions.prompt: Spring k=1000 N/m stretched 0.02 m. Force?
Present worth of $2000 in 3 yr at 10%:
Moment of 80 N at 0.25 m perpendicular:
COP of fridge: 3000 J removed, 750 J work:
